Q1: I tried to keep a consistent routine, usually studying about 8 hours a week spread across a few sessions. Being an auditory learner, I'd often listen to lecture recordings while commuting or doing chores. I'd set aside time in the evenings after work, maybe two or three nights a week, and then catch up more on weekends if I'd fallen behind. It wasn't always perfect, but I tried to maintain some structure.
Q2: My pattern definitely varied depending on what was due. I'd try to keep regular times, but honestly, deadlines would push me to study more intensively right before submissions. With six online courses going on, I had to juggle priorities constantly. Some weeks I'd be more consistent, other weeks it was all about putting out fires and meeting the most urgent deadlines first.
Q3: Usually I'd log in when I knew there was new content posted or when an assignment deadline was approaching. I also got into the habit of checking the discussion forums because I found the peer interactions helpful. Sometimes I'd just log in to see if there were any announcements or updates from instructors. The notification emails would often prompt me to check in too.
Q4: Yeah, after the first few weeks, logging in became pretty automatic for me. I'd check the LMS in the morning with my coffee, almost like checking email or news. The discussion posts helped make it feel more like a community, so I wanted to stay connected. Once I started participating regularly in forums, it felt natural to keep coming back.
Q5: The video lectures were the most valuable for me since I learn best by listening. I'd watch them multiple times sometimes, really letting the explanations sink in. The discussion forums were also great because hearing other students' perspectives helped clarify concepts I was struggling with. I logged over 200 minutes of video watching because that format just clicks with my brain.
Q6: I tended to skip some of the reading materials, especially the really dense PDFs. With my auditory learning style, reading long texts felt like more work for less payoff. I'd sometimes skim them or ignore them if there was a video covering the same content. If something wasn't required or didn't seem directly connected to assignments, I'd often put it aside.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if I could see a clear connection to what I'd be assessed on or if it genuinely helped me understand something I was confused about. I also valued activities where I could engage with other students because that social element kept me motivated. If something felt like busywork without a clear purpose, I'd struggle to prioritize it.
Q8: Optional activities definitely felt different. I'd approach them with a 'if I have time' mentality rather than treating them as essential. Required activities had a sense of urgency attached to them. With six courses to manage, I had to be strategic about where I put my energy, so optional stuff often got pushed aside even when I knew it might be helpful.
Q9: Absolutely, a lot of my studying happened offline. I'd download lecture recordings and listen to them on my phone during my commute or while exercising. I also had some external podcasts and YouTube videos that covered similar topics. Sometimes I'd record myself summarizing concepts and play those back later. None of that showed up in the LMS at all.
Q10: There were many times I'd be reviewing notes I'd taken by hand or listening to downloaded audio files while walking around. I also spent time in study groups with other students where we'd discuss concepts verbally, which suits my learning style. All that productive study time wouldn't register anywhere in the system because it happened completely outside the platform.
Q11: Yes, frequently. I'd watch a video once on the LMS, then download the audio or take detailed notes and work from those offline. For exam prep especially, I'd review my own summaries rather than rewatching everything online. It was more efficient for me to study from materials I'd already processed and personalized.
Q12: Around midterms, things got really tough. I was managing six courses simultaneously, and the workload just piled up. I felt stretched thin trying to keep up with everything. My exam score ended up being lower than I wanted because I couldn't give each course the deep attention it deserved. Those weeks were definitely the hardest to stay engaged.
Q13: Definitely. When I was exhausted or overwhelmed, I'd log into the LMS but just skim things without really absorbing them. Competing responsibilities from work and personal life meant I sometimes had to make tough choices about which course got my attention. Fatigue especially affected my ability to engage meaningfully with complex material late in the evening.
Q14: Yeah, there were times I'd have videos playing but my mind was elsewhere, or I'd scroll through materials without really processing them. It's like being physically present but mentally checked out. After a long day, I'd try to study but realize I hadn't retained anything. Those sessions felt unproductive even though they showed up as activity.
Q15: I'd sometimes postpone the more challenging assignments, especially ones that required deep concentration. I knew they were important, but I'd tell myself I'd do them when I had more energy or focus. With six courses, there was always something more urgent demanding attention. It wasn't avoidance exactly, more like strategic delay that sometimes backfired.
Q16: Deadlines were my main driver for LMS activity. I'd definitely log in more frequently as due dates approached. My assignment completion rate was high because I took deadlines seriously, but my engagement would spike right before submissions. The structure of deadlines helped me prioritize, though it also meant some last-minute scrambling.
Q17: Feedback definitely motivated me to engage more. When I got good marks on assignments, it encouraged me to keep putting in effort. If feedback pointed out areas to improve, I'd go back and review related materials. The problem was feedback sometimes came too late to help with the next assignment, so the timing mattered a lot.
Q18: The course structure with regular deadlines helped me stay on track since I needed that external accountability. Having discussion forums encouraged me to participate and connect with peers. However, having six courses simultaneously made it hard to engage deeply with any single one. The structure was good, but the volume was challenging.
Q19: The video content made engagement easier for me as an auditory learner. The discussion forums also helped because I could learn from others' questions and explanations. What made it harder was when materials were only available as text-heavy PDFs or when the course expected a lot of independent reading. More audio options would have helped me tremendously.
Q20: I think students might be exploring, trying to understand what's expected, or looking for resources without knowing exactly what they need. They could also be struggling with the material and spending time trying to figure things out without successfully completing work. Sometimes high activity is actually a sign of confusion rather than productive engagement.
Q21: That resonates with me somewhat. I did a lot of studying offline that wouldn't show in my LMS logs. Someone could download materials once and study extensively from those resources. The LMS only captures one slice of learning activity. Though in my case, my exam score didn't reflect my effort, which is frustrating.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns like when you log in, what you click on, and how long you spend on videos. They can track submission rates and forum participation. But they miss the quality of engagement, offline studying, external resources used, and whether someone actually understood the material. They capture behavior but not learning.
Q23: Being engaged means actively trying to understand and apply the material, not just going through the motions. It's about caring enough to ask questions, discuss with peers, and connect concepts to real life. For me, engagement includes listening deeply to lectures, participating in discussions, and putting genuine effort into assignments. It's mental investment, not just time spent.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how much I learned through listening to recordings offline or discussing concepts with classmates outside the platform. It wouldn't capture the mindfulness practices I used to manage stress and stay focused. It also wouldn't show that despite failing the exam, I genuinely tried hard and learned a lot that just didn't translate to test performance. My effort was real even if my grade doesn't reflect it.
